lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market manufacturers that repeatedly produce and supply compressed, liquefied, or solid industrial gases to external customers through on-site, merchant/bulk, or packaged-cylinder delivery.
lens.excluded_activities: Pure distributors without gas-manufacturing responsibility, captive internal gas plants, integrated units outside the LMM band, equipment-only vendors, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring B2B product and cylinder-use revenue under purchase orders and multi-year supply agreements; larger on-site customers may have minimum purchases and indexed pass-through, while merchant and packaged customers receive repeated deliveries or pickups.

a.bridge: BLS measures occupations across all chemical manufacturing, not industrial-gas manufacturers, and provides headcount rather than task exposure. I map portions of route and production planning, dispatch, cylinder tracking, procurement, sales, billing, compliance documentation, engineering analysis, maintenance triage, and customer service to AI-addressable tasks, while leaving plant operation, cylinder handling, driving, testing, filling, maintenance, and safety response mostly unexposed. Regional gas firms may have more logistics and customer-service labor than the broader chemical population, so the proxy may bias exposure downward.
a.rationale: Industrial gas combines automated production with labor-intensive distribution, cylinder management, technical service, and commercial administration. AI can optimize information and decisions across the network, but the physical delivery and safety-critical work remains substantial.
a.change_evidence: Payroll-weighted occupational rosters and task diaries from 10-15 independent U.S. industrial-gas manufacturers, split among plant, driver, cylinder, dispatch, commercial, engineering, and administrative roles, would materially change exposure.
caveats: No six-digit wage-weighted task inventory was available.
The estimate excludes process and routing automation already in place.

rho.rationale: Route planning, forecasting, cylinder records, quoting, billing, customer support, maintenance prioritization, and document workflows are implementable within five years. Industrial controls and safety decisions require reliable integration, explainability, cybersecurity, and human accountability; NIST identifies these as material industrial AI barriers.
rho.change_evidence: Before/after studies from regional gas operators showing driver miles, delivery stops, stockouts, cylinder loss, plant uptime, maintenance hours, order-to-cash labor, and safety outcomes would materially move implementation expectations.
caveats: No representative LMM industrial-gas AI deployment study was found.
The range excludes autonomous hazardous-material transport and unreviewed plant control.

e.rationale: Recurring external supply is inherent in the industry: on-site, merchant, and packaged gases are delivered under customer requirements and contracts. Most LMM manufacturers should qualify, with exclusions for captive plants, misclassified pure distributors, equipment-only vendors, and firms lacking independent transferable operations.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level audit of the 24 injected LMM-band firms using production assets, NAICS establishment detail, gas output, ownership, and external recurring revenue would directly establish eligibility.
caveats: Eligibility is inferred from the business model rather than observed for the injected firm list.
Some regional firms commonly described as gas companies may only distribute gas produced by a major.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures five-year sale or transfer intentions among U.S. employer-business owners across industries, not completed industrial-gas control deals; 22% planned to sell or transfer. I keep a similar base because regional gas plants, cylinders, routes, contracts, and customers are transferable and strategic acquirers exist, while environmental/safety diligence and family or internal transfers prevent every intention from qualifying. Net error direction is uncertain.
s5.rationale: The industry has tangible assets and recurring customer agreements that support transferability. Dense regional networks can interest strategic buyers, but specialized facilities, permits, cylinder ownership records, and accident liabilities complicate processes.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook, Capital IQ, Axial, gas-industry broker and strategic-acquirer records for completed and failed U.S. regional industrial-gas transactions, joined to owner age and succession plans, would materially change the estimate.
caveats: The survey population is broader and generally smaller than the LMM lens.
Owner intentions are not observed qualifying control transfers.

q.bridge: Linde is a global major rather than an LMM manufacturer, but it discloses the relevant contract structures: on-site prices indexed with long-term minimums, merchant contracts typically three to seven years, and packaged-gas contracts one to three years. I infer that productivity savings remain with operators inside contract terms and are partly shared at renewal; Linde's scale and network advantage likely bias retention upward relative to regional independents.
q.rationale: Energy pass-through and price indices separate large input-cost movements from internal productivity, while route density, installed tanks, cylinder fleets, safety qualification, and reliable supply create switching friction. Shorter packaged contracts and local competition still allow renewal repricing.
q.change_evidence: A customer-contract panel covering escalators, pass-through, cylinder rent, delivery charges, minimums, renewal prices, churn, and gross margins before and after verified productivity improvements would materially change retention.
caveats: Large-company contract terms may not represent regional LMM firms.
The source does not directly measure retained AI benefit.

d5.bridge: Linde reported 1% Americas volume growth in 2025 and serves diversified end markets; EIA forecasts U.S. industrial natural-gas consumption growth of 1.2% in 2026 and 1.7% in 2027, indicating modest downstream industrial activity. Neither source measures five-year constant-quality U.S. industrial-gas quantity for LMM firms. I use about 1.2% annual base growth, tempered for cyclicality and small-firm share risk; global-major project wins may bias upward.
d5.rationale: Oxygen, nitrogen, argon, carbon dioxide, hydrogen, helium, acetylene, and specialty gases serve healthcare, food, electronics, manufacturing, chemicals, energy, metals, and mining. Diverse end markets and new projects support modest growth, while efficiency and cyclical manufacturing constrain the downside and upside.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year U.S. industrial-gas volume forecasts by molecule and delivery mode, reconciled to regional producer shipment histories, electronics projects, hospital demand, metals output, and plant closures, would materially change the range.
caveats: Linde is global and much larger than the lens; Americas includes countries beyond the United States.
Natural-gas consumption is a downstream-activity proxy, not industrial-gas demand.

o.rationale: Nearly all year-five gas quantity still requires an accountable operator to manufacture, compress or liquefy, test, store, fill, track, and safely deliver the physical product. Software can optimize ordering and logistics, but it cannot replace the gas molecule or regulated custody; limited customer on-site generation or self-service pickup can shift supply mode without eliminating manufacturing.
o.change_evidence: Customer procurement data on in-sourced generation, membrane/PSA on-site systems, direct-from-major sourcing, additive-manufacturing process changes, and gas-intensity reduction would materially change persistence.
caveats: Operator persistence is inferred from physical delivery and plant obligations rather than directly measured.
On-site generation can displace merchant or packaged suppliers even when aggregate gas use persists.

narrative.executive_view: Industrial gas manufacturing closely matches the recurring external-supply lens: customers repeatedly consume physical gases under contracts, and local production/distribution networks matter. AI can improve logistics, cylinder control, planning, and administration, while accountable production and hazardous delivery remain indispensable.
narrative.ai_impact: The strongest applications are demand forecasting, route and load optimization, cylinder identification and exception management, dispatch, predictive maintenance, engineering support, safety-document retrieval, quoting, billing, collections, and customer service. Human operators, drivers, technicians, and release authorities remain central to plants, cylinders, field installations, and emergencies.
narrative.value_capture: Multi-year agreements, indexed energy pass-through, installed tanks, cylinder fleets, route density, and supply-critical customer processes support retention. Packaged-gas purchase orders and shorter contracts expose regional firms to renewal pricing and local competitors, so savings are not fully protected.
narrative.firm_availability: Most true LMM manufacturers should be eligible, but some nominal regional gas companies are distributors rather than producers. Tangible assets, routes, contracts, and customers make firms transferable, while permits, cylinder records, safety history, and environmental exposure demand careful diligence.
narrative.demand_durability: Diverse end markets provide resilience, with electronics, healthcare, food, manufacturing, metals, and chemicals requiring recurring gases. Cyclical industrial volume and customer-installed generation are the main quantity risks, not software elimination.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Misclassifying distributors as manufacturers is the central eligibility risk. Catastrophic safety events, energy cost, helium availability, cylinder loss, fleet compliance, plant outages, customer concentration, major-company competition, and capital intensity can overwhelm labor savings.
narrative.principal_driver: Recurring consumption, multi-year supply relationships, and regional delivery economics create durable operator-required revenue.
narrative.principal_weakness: Manufacturing eligibility within the LMM population is unverified, and much of the contract evidence comes from a global major.

sources.supports: U.S. employer-business five-year sale and transfer intentions.

sources.supports: Near-term U.S. industrial activity and chemical-sector natural-gas demand as a downstream demand proxy.
